reasoning_model_1: The model was tasked with reading the sentence with a 'loudly volume'. The generated audio successfully fulfills all aspects of the prompt. The content is perfectly accurate, matching the script 'Happy New Year everyone! May all your wishes come true!'. The speech is fluent and free of any artifacts. Most importantly, the paralinguistic requirement is met perfectly. Throughout the entire audio (0:00 - 0:05), the speaker uses a high volume and an energetic, projected tone, as if shouting a celebratory message to a crowd. The expression is vivid and immediately conveys the intended loudness.
reasoning_model_2: The model was instructed to read the sentence with a 'loudly volume'. While the audio quality itself is good, it fails on the core requirement. The content accuracy is perfect, and the speech is very fluent and natural. However, the model completely ignored the instruction for a 'loudly volume'. The entire sentence (0:00 - 0:05) is spoken at a normal, conversational volume. There is no attempt to sound loud, projected, or shouting. The tone is cheerful, but the specified paralinguistic feature of volume is entirely absent.
model_comparison: Both models produced audio with perfect content accuracy and excellent fluency. The primary and decisive difference is in their adherence to the paralinguistic command for a 'loudly volume'. Model 1 executed the command flawlessly; its delivery is audibly loud, projected, and energetic from start to finish (0:00 - 0:05), perfectly matching the instruction. Model 2, on the other hand, completely disregarded the command, producing a standard, conversational-volume reading of the text. While its tone is pleasant, the absence of the requested loudness is a critical failure in feature control. This difference is significant, as Model 1 demonstrated the ability to control the specified feature while Model 2 did not. Therefore, Model 1 is unequivocally superior.
